title: Writing - Part 1: Youth and Independence
description: Your class has been discussing at what age young people should be given more independence.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the aspects in your notes. You should explain which aspect you think is most important when deciding how much independence young people should have, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Young people learn best when they are allowed to make their own decisions.
Too much freedom too young can lead to poor choices and serious mistakes.
Independence builds self-confidence and prepares young people for adult life.

structure.introduction: Introduce the question of independence for young people and the two aspects you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first aspect (e.g., personal responsibility and maturity) and why it matters.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second aspect (e.g., the importance of making mistakes) and how it aids development.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both aspects and state which you think is most important when deciding on independence.

content.modelAnswer:
The question of how much freedom young people should be given is one that parents, educators and young people themselves often debate. Striking the right balance between protection and independence is challenging but essential for healthy development. This essay will consider the role of personal maturity and the value of learning from mistakes.

A young person's level of maturity and sense of responsibility is a key factor in determining how much independence they should have. A teenager who demonstrates reliable judgement and accepts the consequences of their actions has shown they are ready for more freedom. Rewarding responsible behaviour with greater autonomy helps reinforce positive habits.

However, making mistakes is itself an important part of growing up. When young people are allowed to make their own decisions, they inevitably face setbacks and failures. These experiences, though sometimes painful, build resilience, problem-solving skills and self-awareness. Overprotecting young people can actually prevent them from developing these crucial life skills.

In conclusion, while maturity is an important consideration, I believe the freedom to make mistakes is ultimately more significant for young people's development. No one becomes truly independent without experiencing failure and learning how to recover from it. The role of adults should be to guide rather than prevent, allowing young people to grow through their own experiences.
